metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem or ROI case exists across any source. The two March 2026 intro calls (12 Mar with Carolina Coppolo, 18 Mar with perezr4@metro.net) were high-level operational introductions with no pain articulation, no cost/savings discussion, and no business-case development. SF fields Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c are all empty. LA County Metro is a large public transit agency with ~$8B annual budget, likely seeking to modernize procurement/sourcing/contract management, but the specific pain (manual processes, compliance risk, spend visibility, supplier management inefficiency) and quantified impact (headcount savings, cycle-time reduction, spend under management, compliance cost avoidance) were never surfaced. Without a quantified value case, there is no business justification for the $160k investment and no anchor for commercial negotiation. The move is to run a discovery call with the operational owner (Debra Avila, CPO or senior procurement exec per prior context) to surface the pain, quantify the impact, and build the ROI narrative.

champion.narrative: No champion identified. Zero contact roles in SF (no Champion role assigned). The two March calls involved operational contacts Carolina Coppolo and perezr4@metro.net, but neither demonstrated advocacy, access to power, or active engagement post-intro—both ghosted after the initial meetings. SF fields X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c and Decision_Maker_Identified__c are empty. A champion in a public-sector deal is typically a senior procurement or operational leader who (1) has access to the CFO/EB and decision-making process, (2) actively advocates for Zycus internally (pushes back on competitors, drives the agenda, shares internal docs/timelines), and (3) guides the rep through the approval and contracting process. Neither March contact showed these behaviors—the calls were intros, and no follow-on engagement occurred. The silence suggests either they are not champions (too junior, no influence, or not invested) or they are champion candidates who need development (build access to power, align on mutual win, co-create the business case). The move is to re-engage Carolina or perezr4, assess their influence and access, and if weak, ask for an intro to a senior stakeholder (Debra Avila, Nalini Ahuja) who can champion the deal internally.

competition.narrative: No competitive intelligence. SF field Competitors__c is empty. No competitor named in the March intro calls, no Next_Step_History__c references to rival vendors, and no competitive discussion in any source. For a large public-sector procurement, likely competitors include SAP Ariba (common incumbent in government/transit), Coupa (strong public-sector penetration), GEP, Jaggaer, Oracle Procurement Cloud, and Ivalua. Whether LA County Metro is running a competitive RFP (2-3 vendors shortlisted), sole-sourcing Zycus, evaluating one other vendor, or has an incumbent they are replacing (SF Existing_vendor__c, Replacing_What__c both empty) is unknown. The lack of competitive intel means Zycus cannot position its wedge (Merlin AI for negotiation/sourcing, integrated suite vs point solutions, public-sector references, compliance features) or develop a win strategy. The buyer may be evaluating multiple vendors in parallel and Zycus may be losing ground without visibility. The move is to ask the buyer directly in the re-engagement call: 'Are you evaluating other vendors? Is there an incumbent system we would be replacing?' and position Zycus's strengths against the likely competitors (Merlin AI differentiation, full-suite integration, transit/public-sector case studies).

paper_process.narrative: No paper process documented. No discussion of MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel (direct vs through an SI/reseller), or InfoSec requirements in the March calls or SF fields. SF field Mandate__c is empty. Public-sector procurements typically require (1) formal vendor registration, (2) compliance certifications (e.g., California Public Contract Code compliance, insurance certificates, conflict-of-interest disclosures), (3) board or executive approval above a threshold, (4) MSA and SOW negotiation, (5) legal review, and (6) InfoSec/IT review for SaaS deployments. Whether LA County Metro has these requirements, and what the typical contracting timeline is (30-90 days post-award), is unknown. SF Current_Contract_Expiration__c is null, so no incumbent-contract forcing function exists. The move is to ask the operational contact or procurement lead (Debra Avila) what the procurement and contracting requirements are, and build a timeline backward from the Feb 2027 close date.

flags: RE-ENGAGE the relevant stakeholder (highest priority): 95 days since creation, zero buyer calls, no verified activity. This is not a sales cycle; it is a static record. The deal owner must reach out directly to the buyer contact roster (Debra Avila, Selena Landero, Fred Origel, James Sheridan) within 7 days to confirm whether LA County MTA has a live procurement process for eProcurement/CLM/sourcing. If no active process, disqualify and close-lost to clean the pipeline. If live, map the buying committee and run discovery immediately.

MULTI-THREAD AND MAP POWER (if live): Zero contact roles on the opportunity. For a $160k public-sector deal spanning procurement, contract management, and sourcing, we need the Economic Buyer (likely Nalini Ahuja, CFO, or a procurement executive), the Decision Maker (procurement lead), and a Champion (internal advocate). Add confirmed stakeholders to the opportunity as contact roles and build access to power.

RUN MEDDPICC DISCOVERY (if live): All MEDDPICC elements are gaps. Schedule a discovery call within 14 days to establish the business case (quantified pain, savings target, cycle-time improvement), confirm decision criteria (RFP/RFI requirements, AI/Merlin appetite), map the approval process (board/legal/procurement workflow), and identify the champion. Without this foundation, the deal cannot progress.

Map Economic Buyer and Decision Maker during discovery. Zero contact roles and zero power mapping is a fatal gap for a $160k public-sector deal. For LA County Metro, the Economic Buyer is likely a CFO-level executive (Nalini Ahuja, Executive Director CFO, or Jesse Soto, Executive Officer Finance/Controller) or a senior procurement executive (Debra Avila, Chief Vendor/Contract Management Officer, or Selena Landero, Supply Chain Management & Logistics Executive Officer). The Decision Maker is likely the same or a board-level approval for contracts above a threshold. Without EB/DM identification, there is no path to commercials, no budget confirmation, and no ability to close. The discovery call must map the power structure and secure introductions to the Economic Buyer.

Position Merlin AI and public-sector differentiation. The product scope includes Merlin (AI-powered negotiation, contract intelligence, sourcing optimization), which is a strong differentiator vs traditional procurement platforms (SAP Ariba, Coupa, Oracle). Public-sector entities like LA County Metro face compliance pressure, budget scrutiny, supplier-diversity mandates, and audit requirements — areas where Zycus has deep reference customers (California agencies, federal contractors, large municipalities). The discovery call should lead with AI-driven savings and compliance automation, supported by public-sector references, to differentiate Zycus from incumbents and establish credibility before the RFP process (if one exists) advances.
